epigraph.text: Do not judge by appearances, but judge with right judgment.
epigraph.quote: The chi-square test compares observed and expected frequencies.

content.text:
Chi-square tests analyze categorical data, testing whether observed 
frequencies match expected patterns.

content.title: Chi-Square Goodness-of-Fit
content.content:
To test whether data fit a hypothesized distribution:
\[
 \chi^2 = \sum \frac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}
\]
where $O_i$ = observed count, $E_i$ = expected count.

Under $H_0$, $\chi^2 \approx \chi^2_{k-1}$ where $k$ = number of categories.

content.title: Goodness-of-Fit
content.problem:
A die is rolled 120 times with results: 1(25), 2(17), 3(22), 4(18), 
5(21), 6(17). Test if the die is fair.
content.solution:
Expected: 20 each.

$\chi^2 = \frac{(25-20)^2}{20} + \frac{(17-20)^2}{20} + \cdots = 3.7$

$df = 5$, critical value at $\alpha = 0.05$ is 11.07.

$3.7 < 11.07$: fail to reject $H_0$. No evidence die is unfair.

content.title: Chi-Square Test of Independence
content.content:
For a contingency table with $r$ rows and $c$ columns:
\[
 \chi^2 = \sum \frac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}
\]
where $E_{ij} = \frac{(\text{row } i \text{ total})(\text{column } j \text{ total})}{\text{grand total}}$

$df = (r-1)(c-1)$

items: Expected counts should be at least 5 (rule of thumb)
